slug: 为什么老房子比新房子耐震_有时候
title: 为什么老房子比新房子耐震——有时候

outline.concept: Accidental overstrength: old builders couldn't precisely calculate loads, so they overbuilt massively. A timber beam sized by eye or tradition ends up 3-5x stronger than needed — not because they were smarter, but because they couldn't afford to be precise.
outline.query: 老房子 厚墙 粗梁 超出需要 overbuilt 安全余量

outline.concept: Ductility beats strength in earthquakes: structures that can deform without collapsing survive better than rigid ones that snap. Old timber frames and lime-mortar masonry flex and crack in small ways that dissipate seismic energy — they bend, they don't break.
outline.query: 木材 弯曲 变形 没断 flexible 裂而不倒 吸收能量

outline.concept: The modern brittle trap: reinforced concrete that's under-reinforced or poorly detailed is rigid until catastrophic failure. A 'strong' concrete column with insufficient ties doesn't bend — it explodes. Some new construction is strong but brittle, which is the worst combo in a quake.
outline.query: 混凝土 突然断裂 脆性 钢筋不够 瞬间倒塌 brittle

outline.concept: Survivorship bias distorts the comparison: we only see old houses that survived past earthquakes. The ones that collapsed are gone and forgotten. The real lesson isn't 'old is better' — it's that ductility (deformation capacity) matters more than raw strength, and modern seismic design actually builds on this principle.
outline.query: 倒塌 看不到了 幸存者偏差 survivorship 只看到没倒的
